title: Source lineage for The Lay of Havelok the Dane
summary: The checked-in aha2626 XML is a copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item AHA2626.0001.001. That electronic item encodes Walter W. Skeat's 1868 edition of the Middle English poem on printed pp. 1–86. Skeat described his volume as a revised re-edition of Frederic Madden's 1828 first edition, retained Madden's text as his editorial base, checked proofs against Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 twice, and incorporated Madden's later manuscript corrections. Madden had transcribed the manuscript directly. Laud Misc. 108, ff. 204ra–219va, is the sole substantially complete witness; one leaf is missing after f. 211 and conventional lines 1445–1624 are lost. Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19) consists of four physical paper scraps, three of which preserve collateral portions of Havelok; Skeat first published those fragments in 1911, so they are not upstream of either the 1828 or 1868 edition. A complete Digital Bodleian facsimile of Laud is cached, and its exact Havelok range is mapped within the whole manuscript. No public facsimile of the complete Cambridge fragment item was found in a dated scoped search; Cambridge Imaging Services can be asked to supply all four scraps and eight surfaces. Catalogs, repertories, modern scholarship, scans, OCR derivatives, access conditions, and rights layers are modeled separately from the physical and textual transmission path.

entities.label: Anonymous Middle English The Lay of Havelok the Dane
entities.description: The conceptual anonymous Middle English verse romance, distinct from its physical witnesses and later editions, encodings, scans, and descriptions. DIMEV identifies it as record 1795 / IMEV-NIMEV 1114 and gives the conventional extent as 3,001 lines in rhyming couplets.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.value: End of the thirteenth century; precise dating disputed
date_statements.notes: This work date must not be inferred from the broader date of the Laud codex or the dates of its hands.

notes: The related Anglo-Norman lai and Gaimar episode belong to the wider Havelok tradition, but no particular French manuscript is asserted as the English poem's physical exemplar.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2626
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The carrier contains four DIV1 elements, 222 PB, 223 EPB, 97 LG, 2,827 L, 135 NOTE1, 63 MILESTONE, and eight P elements.

Of 2,823 direct LG/L positions, two are asterisk-only lacuna rows; the remaining 2,821 are extant verbal medieval verse lines. Four additional L elements occur inside two editorial notes and are not part of the direct poem stream.

The conventional line sequence reaches 3,001 while lines 1445–1624 are absent because of the lost Laud leaf. The 97 LG containers represent Skeat's editorial paragraph or display groups, not 97 intrinsic stanzas.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2626.0001.001
entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR-derived TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 electronic text encoding Skeat's title page and Middle English poem, retaining page-break links and omission placeholders for unencoded carrier matter.
notes: The encoding carries Skeat's 1868 printed page sequence and direct poem text, not a new direct transcription of either manuscript witness.
Its line stream does not incorporate the eleven additional lines published from the Cambridge fragments in 1911.

entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, The Lay of Havelok the Dane (1868)
entities.description: The immediate print source of the U-M encoding: an EETS re-edition of Madden's 1828 volume whose English poem Skeat also checked directly against Laud Misc. 108 twice.

bibliographic.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Lay of Havelok the Dane, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 4 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868).
bibliographic.title: The Lay of Havelok the Dane
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 4

notes: Skeat's direct manuscript work and his scoped inheritance from Madden are modeled separately; neither relation should be erased in a flattened one-parent chain.
Skeat omitted the complete Anglo-Norman lai and Gaimar text printed by Madden, retaining only selected comparison passages in notes.

entities.label: Frederic Madden, The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane (1828)
entities.description: The first printed edition of the Middle English poem, transcribed directly from Laud Misc. 108 and published with an introduction, French analogues, notes, and glossary.

bibliographic.citation: Frederic Madden, ed., The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane, accompanied by the French Text, with an Introduction, Notes, and a Glossary (London: W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, for the Roxburghe Club, 1828).
bibliographic.title: The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane
bibliographic.publisher: W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, for the Roxburghe Club
bibliographic.pages: English-poem section pp. 1–104; poem text pp. 3–104

notes: Madden reports permission to transcribe Laud and says he adhered scrupulously to its orthography while supplying punctuation, word division, capitalization, obvious letter corrections, and modern equivalents for specified archaic letterforms.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: The sole substantially complete witness of Havelok, copied by hand C at ff. 204ra–219va. One lost leaf after f. 211 removes conventional lines 1445–1624. King Horn begins later in the same f. 219va column.

date_statements.label: containing codex
date_statements.notes: The Havelok/King Horn hand is more narrowly described as late thirteenth or early fourteenth century.

holding.notes: Havelok occupies ff. 204ra–219va; the containing production unit continues with King Horn.

physical_description.extent: Havelok at ff. 204ra–219va within a complete composite codex; one leaf missing after f. 211
physical_description.format: Two columns, normally 45 lines; hand C
notes: The Havelok/King Horn scribe's language is associated with west Norfolk; this is witness dialect, not the poem's Northeast Midlands composition localization.
The codex's possible Oxford origin is tentative and must not be promoted to the work's place of composition.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19)
entities.description: A binding-fragment item consisting of four physical paper scraps and eight surfaces, conventionally lettered a–h. Three scraps preserve Havelok text on surfaces d, e, and f; the fourth physical scrap is part of the complete item but does not carry Havelok.

date_statements.label: fragment witness
date_statements.notes: Skeat's 1911 end-of-fourteenth-century estimate is retained as publication history but superseded here by the modern cataloging date.

holding.notes: Request the complete fragment item—all four scraps and both sides—not only the three Havelok-bearing scraps.

physical_description.extent: Four physical scraps / eight surfaces; Havelok survives on three scraps, surfaces d–f
notes: DIMEV maps d to conventional lines 174–183, e to 341–364, and f to 537–549.
Skeat's transcription preserves eleven verse lines absent from the Laud witness: ten after line 544 and one in the following passage.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: The complete 496-canvas Digital Bodleian surrogate. Havelok occupies canvases 423–454; canvas 454 is shared with the beginning of King Horn.
entities.reproduction_format: 496 color IIIF canvases; locally cached at the 1600-pixel image profile

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Skeat's 1868 edition
entities.description: A complete scan of the printed edition from which the U-M encoded poem can be checked at printed pp. 1–86 / PDF pages 69–154.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Madden's 1828 edition
entities.description: A complete scan of Madden's volume, including the English poem, French analogues, introduction, notes, and glossary.

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR of Madden's 1828 edition
entities.description: Machine-readable OCR of the complete scanned volume; useful for search, but not authoritative over the scan or printed edition.

entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, ‘A New Havelok MS.’ (1911)
entities.description: The first publication and full transcription of the Cambridge Havelok scraps d–f, appearing decades after the 1868 edition and serving as collateral witness evidence rather than a source of aha2626.

bibliographic.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ‘A New Havelok MS.,’ The Modern Language Review 6, no. 4 (1911): 455–457.
bibliographic.title: A New Havelok MS.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Modern Language Review 6.4 (1911)
entities.description: A complete issue scan containing Skeat's article at printed pp. 455–457 / PDF pages 23–25.

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR of Modern Language Review 6.4 (1911)
entities.description: OCR of the complete issue, including a searchable derivative transcription of Skeat's Havelok-fragment article.

entities.label: Bodleian catalog description of MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: The current official manuscript description, separately modeled from the medieval codex and its photographic surrogate.

entities.label: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 record 1795
entities.description: A version-pinned verse repertory record identifying the work, conventional extent, and both Laud and Cambridge witnesses.

entities.label: Database of Middle English Romance record for Havelok the Dane
entities.description: A specialist romance repertory record providing work metadata, plot summary, and separate descriptions of both witnesses.

entities.label: Bell and Couch, The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (2011)
entities.description: An open-access scholarly essay collection about the complete codex and its vernacular narratives. It contains supporting scholarship about Havelok but not an edition or facsimile of the target medieval poem.

bibliographic.citation: Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108: The Shaping of English Vernacular Narrative (Leiden and Boston: Brill, 2011).
bibliographic.title: The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108

entities.label: King Horn
entities.description: The distinct Middle English romance copied immediately after Havelok by the same Laud hand. It begins in f. 219va, the same column in which Havelok ends, and is included only to make the shared physical boundary explicit.

access.access_method: Submit the Cambridge Imaging Services online form or email photo@lib.cam.ac.uk. Request the complete physical item: all four scraps, all eight surfaces, housing or envelope, edges, and a scale or color target where offered—not only the three Havelok-bearing scraps.
access.format: Professional digital images; exact specification and licence by quotation
notes: The published minimum charge is £25 excluding VAT; Cambridge states that quotations are normally supplied within about two weeks.
Images are initially supplied for noncommercial research, private study, or internal evaluation; reproduction requires prior written permission and a specific licence.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where the medieval copyright term has expired
rights.basis: The work was composed in the medieval period and is far outside any subsisting copyright term; this conclusion does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, descriptions, scans, or photographs.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where the medieval copyright term has expired
rights.basis: The medieval verbal work is outside any subsisting copyright term; its presence here only documents the shared Laud boundary.

rights.component: Madden's 1828 editorial text, introduction, notes, and glossary
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1828 and is outside any possible United States copyright term.
notes: This status does not automatically license every later scan derivative or provider metadata record.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1868 and is outside any possible United States copyright term.
notes: This status does not automatically determine rights in the Internet Archive scan derivative.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1911 and is outside any possible United States copyright term.
notes: The conclusion applies to the historical article, not automatically to later scan and OCR derivatives.

rights.component: U-M OCR correction state, TEI markup, page-break links, and electronic compilation
rights.basis: U-M describes the collection as electronic texts created from public-domain editions, but the exact item rights statement could not be retrieved during this review and no item-specific licence was verified for the encoding layer.
notes: The public-domain status of the medieval work and 1868 edition must not be silently transferred to new U-M markup or compilation features.

rights.component: Checked-in XML artifact as a copy of the U-M encoding
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense file phrased for software, but the review did not find an explicit statement that it licenses the imported U-M XML corpus or overrides any upstream encoding rights.
notes: Treat the medieval verbal text and historical edition as public domain while keeping the copied encoding layer's status explicit and unresolved.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan derivative and item files
rights.basis: The exact Internet Archive metadata record applies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the item; the underlying 1828 edition is public domain in the United States.
The OCR is a derivative file within the exact Internet Archive item marked with Public Domain Mark 1.0; exact readings remain non-authoritative because this is machine OCR.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan derivative and item files
rights.basis: The historical edition is public domain, but the exact Internet Archive metadata record supplies no licence URL or item-specific rights statement for the scan derivative.
Skeat's 1911 article is public domain in the United States, but the exact Internet Archive issue metadata contains no licence URL or item-specific rights statement for the scan derivative.
The underlying 1911 article is public domain, but no item-specific licence was found for the OCR derivative in the exact issue metadata.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and IIIF image service
rights.basis: Digital Bodleian applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 to the digital images and requires compliance with its attribution and reuse terms.
notes: The noncommercial condition is incompatible with unlicensed commercial or print-on-demand redistribution of the facsimile images; request permission for such use.
Digital Bodleian separately permits specified academic uses within its terms, but those terms should be consulted for the exact proposed publication.

rights.component: Bodleian catalog prose and TEI encoding
rights.basis: No explicit open licence for the exact catalog prose or cached TEI record was verified during this review.
The exact Zenodo release declares Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.

rights.component: Database of Middle English Romance catalog prose, plot summary, and data
rights.basis: The site supplies scholarly descriptive content, and no open-content licence for the exact records was found during this review.
notes: Facts may be cited and independently restated; do not copy the site's plot prose or database expression wholesale without permission.

rights.component: Bell and Couch 2011 book and individual chapters
rights.basis: The open-access volume and its chapters state Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 reuse terms.

rights.component: Any professional images supplied by Cambridge Imaging Services
rights.basis: The item has not yet been ordered, so no image-specific licence exists in this record. Cambridge states that supplied images are initially for noncommercial research, private study, or internal evaluation and that reproduction requires prior written permission and a specific licence.
notes: Record the exact quotation and licence before redistributing any supplied images in the repository, a publication, or a commercial print-on-demand product.

editorial_practices.representation: Editor supplied punctuation and word division while claiming otherwise scrupulous adherence to manuscript orthography
editorial_practices.description: Madden explicitly distinguishes his supplied punctuation and word division from the manuscript forms he sought to preserve.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English poem, English section pp. 3–104

editorial_practices.representation: Editor supplied capitalization and modern equivalents for specified archaic letterforms, and corrected letters judged obvious errors
editorial_practices.description: The printed edition is deliberately close to Laud but not a diplomatic facsimile; Madden lists these interventions in his introduction.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English poem, English section pp. 3–104

editorial_practices.source_feature: Madden's 1828 base text and Laud Misc. 108
editorial_practices.representation: Skeat corrected the retained Madden base from Madden's fresh comparisons and by reading proof sheets against Laud twice
editorial_practices.description: This is manuscript collation of a retained prior-edition base, not evidence that Skeat independently retranscribed the continuous poem from scratch.
notes: The lineage therefore uses version_of Madden plus collated_against Laud, not transcribes Laud.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript contractions and the letter wynn
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial expansions are printed in italics; wynn is represented by italic w
editorial_practices.description: Skeat uses typography to expose supplied expansion and letterform decisions relevant to interpreting the italics preserved by the U-M encoding.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Large or decorated initials used by the Laud scribe to mark textual divisions
editorial_practices.representation: Printed paragraph or display groups, represented as 97 LG containers in the U-M XML
editorial_practices.description: Skeat states that paragraph divisions follow manuscript large initials. The highly variable 4–176-line groups should not be reclassified as 97 intrinsic stanzas.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English poem, printed pp. 1–86 / U-M BODY poem

editorial_practices.source_feature: Madden's complete volume includes the Middle English poem, Anglo-Norman lai, Gaimar episode, introduction, notes, and glossary
editorial_practices.representation: Skeat re-edits the English poem and scoped apparatus but omits the complete French texts, retaining selected comparison passages in notes
editorial_practices.description: The 1868 volume is a selective revised edition, not a complete reproduction of Madden's 1828 carrier.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Skeat's complete 1868 volume with preface, poem, notes, glossary, and indexes
editorial_practices.representation: The title page and Middle English poem are encoded; other front and back matter is represented by omission placeholders and page-break scaffolding
editorial_practices.description: The electronic carrier is an uncorrected OCR-derived Level 1 encoding of a selected part of the print volume, not a complete digital edition of all Skeat's matter.
notes: The direct poem stream has 2,821 extant verbal lines plus two asterisk-only lacuna rows; four conjectural verse lines nested in two notes are editorial apparatus.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Cambridge Add. 4407 (19) consists of four physical scraps / eight surfaces
editorial_practices.representation: The article transcribes the three Havelok-bearing scraps d–f and describes their relationship to Laud lineation
editorial_practices.description: The article is a collateral fragment transcription. It does not supply a facsimile of the complete four-scrap physical item and is not a source of the 1868/U-M text.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2626.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; HEADER, FRONT, TITLEPAGE, BODY poem, inline NOTE1 elements, and BACK
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for artifact identity and fixity, AHA2626.0001.001, the 2006 uncorrected OCR and TEI-in-Libraries Level 1 statements, Skeat's 1868 title page and pagination, the selected poem, editorial notes, page links, omitted matter, and XML structure. A target-scoped audit found four DIV1, 222 PB, 223 EPB, 97 LG, 2,827 L, 135 NOTE1, 63 MILESTONE, and eight P elements; 2,823 L are direct LG children, two of those are asterisk-only lacuna rows, 2,821 are extant verbal lines, and four conjectural lines occur inside two editorial notes. Conventional lines 1445–1624 are absent within the sequence ending at 3001.
notes: The 97 LG groups vary from 4 to 176 lines and reproduce editorial paragraph/display spacing; they are not asserted as intrinsic stanzas.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item AHA2626.0001.001.
evidence.locator: stable item identifier and electronic text landing route
evidence.summary: Official stable identifier for the upstream electronic item represented by the pinned XML.
notes: The endpoint returned a Cloudflare interstitial during this review; no item-specific rights text was inferred from that response.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.locator: collection description and access routes
evidence.summary: Collection-level evidence that U-M created electronic texts from historical editions and provides access to the CME corpus; it does not by itself settle the exact TEI-markup rights for AHA2626.0001.001.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Lay of Havelok the Dane, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 4 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868).
evidence.locator: title page; preface §§1–3 and §26; poem pp. 1–86 = PDF pages 69–154; notes pp. 87 onward
evidence.summary: Primary evidence that the 1868 volume is a revised re-edition of Madden, that Madden supplied fresh manuscript corrections and reviewed it, and that Skeat read proofs against Laud twice. It also documents expansion typography, italic wynn, paragraph initials, the 180-line lacuna, selective omission of full French texts, and scoped inheritance of Madden's preface, notes, glossary, and English text.
notes: The evidence supports version_of Madden and collated_against Laud. It does not explicitly say Skeat independently retranscribed the complete continuous text from Laud.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and scandata for item layofhavelokdane00madduoft.
evidence.locator: complete item metadata; file list and item fields
evidence.summary: Exact provider metadata for the cached 232-page Skeat scan. It lists the complete PDF and derivative files but supplies no licence URL or item-specific rights statement. The separately cached scandata is linked and hashed in the access record.

evidence.citation: Frederic Madden, ed., The Ancient English Romance of Havelok the Dane, accompanied by the French Text, with an Introduction, Notes, and a Glossary (London: W. Nicol, Shakespeare Press, for the Roxburghe Club, 1828).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. i–lvi, especially lii–lvi; English-poem section pp. 1–104, poem text pp. 3–104 = PDF pages 69–170
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the 1828 first edition, Madden's permission to transcribe Laud K. 60 / Laud Misc. 108 directly, the stated literal-transcript aim and supplied punctuation, word division, capitalization, obvious corrections, and letterform substitutions. The complete volume also establishes the separate comparative French, note, glossary, and introduction boundaries.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive DjVu OCR derivative of Madden's 1828 Havelok volume.
evidence.locator: complete OCR; English poem section corresponding to printed pp. 3–104
evidence.summary: Searchable OCR of the complete volume. It includes the target poem but is a machine derivative and is not used in place of the page images for exact readings or typography.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and scandata for item TheAncientEnglishRomanceOfHavelokT.
evidence.locator: complete item metadata; identifier, file list, and licenseurl
evidence.summary: Exact provider metadata for the 334-page Madden scan and OCR. It applies the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the item. The separately cached scandata is linked and hashed in the access record.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ‘A New Havelok MS.,’ The Modern Language Review 6, no. 4 (1911): 455–457.
evidence.locator: article pp. 455–457 = PDF pages 23–25
evidence.summary: First publication and full transcription of Havelok-bearing Cambridge scraps d, e, and f, mapping them against the Laud line sequence and recording ten extra lines after 544 plus one further additional line. Its 1911 date places this collateral witness publication outside the 1828/1868/U-M source path.
notes: Skeat's end-of-fourteenth-century dating is a historical assessment; the modern DMER record gives mid-fourteenth century.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive DjVu OCR derivative of Modern Language Review 6.4 (1911).
evidence.locator: complete issue OCR; article ‘A New Havelok MS.’ corresponding to pp. 455–457
evidence.summary: Searchable OCR of the complete issue, including Skeat's fragment transcription. Exact readings are checked against the PDF rather than accepted from OCR alone.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for item modern-language-review_1911-10_6_4.
evidence.locator: complete issue metadata and file list
evidence.summary: Exact provider metadata for the cached 142-page issue PDF and OCR derivative. It supplies no licence URL or item-specific rights statement.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Laud Misc. 108; commit-pinned TEI description.
evidence.locator: Havelok msItem ff. 204ra–219va; King Horn begins f. 219va; quire 19, hand C, language, layout, date, and production-unit descriptions
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the exact Havelok locus; Middle English with Latin rubric; west-Norfolk hand-C dialect; missing quire-19 leaf after f. 211; two-column 45-line layout; 1290–1400 broad codex date; distinct but early-joined Havelok/King Horn unit; shared f. 219va boundary; and restricted original-manuscript consultation.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF facsimile of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108.
evidence.locator: all 496 canvases; Havelok canvases 423–454 / images/000423.jpg–images/000454.jpg / ff. 204r–219v; lacuna boundary canvases 438–439; shared end canvas 454
evidence.summary: The complete facsimile confirms Havelok's rubric and opening, the physical lost-leaf boundary between f. 211v and f. 212r, resumption after conventional line 1624, manuscript initial/display behavior, and Havelok's end before King Horn begins on the shared f. 219v surface.
notes: Deep integrity verification succeeded for all 496 exact inventory images in the cached 1600-pixel bundle.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Terms of Use.
evidence.locator: image licence, attribution, noncommercial condition, academic use, and permission routes
evidence.summary: Official evidence that Digital Bodleian images are made available under CC BY-NC 4.0 and that commercial reuse, including unlicensed commercial publication, requires permission; the terms also identify specified academic uses.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 1795.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-1795 and witnesses wit-1795-1/wit-1795-2; manuscript records BodLauMis108 and CULAdd4407b; licence and citation files
evidence.summary: Version-pinned current census evidence for DIMEV 1795 / IMEV-NIMEV 1114, conventional 3,001-line extent in couplets, the Laud locus, and Cambridge fragment mappings d 174–183, e 341–364, and f 537–549. The exact release declares CC BY 4.0.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is a prerelease; record and witness claims are pinned to this exact archive.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, ‘Havelok the Dane’ and witness records for Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 and Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 D (19).
evidence.locator: work title, date, place, form, plot, IMEV, witnesses, and edition list; linked witness pages for Laud and Cambridge
evidence.summary: Specialist repertory evidence for end-of-thirteenth-century composition, Northeast Midlands localization, aa4 bb4 rhyming couplets, and two witnesses. The Laud page gives 3,001 lines with 1445–1624 missing; the Cambridge page identifies a mid-fourteenth-century Norfolk paper binding-fragment witness. All three substantive server-rendered HTML pages are cached and individually fixed in the access record.
notes: The cached work-page HTML is the object hashed here; separate hashes for both cached witness pages appear under access:dmer-record.
No bot, WAF, or JavaScript-only shell is treated as evidence.

evidence.citation: Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108: The Shaping of English Vernacular Narrative (Leiden and Boston: Brill, 2011).
evidence.locator: introduction pp. 1–20; Thomas R. Liszka pp. 31–50; Andrew Taylor pp. 71–86, especially p. 81; frontmatter and chapter licence notices
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly synthesis for the sole-substantially-complete-witness qualification, composition and manuscript dating debate, Matter of England and hagiographical contexts, the Laud romance production unit, and the Cambridge fragments' Norfolk association. The complete supporting book does not reproduce the target medieval poem and carries CC BY-NC 4.0 notices.

evidence.citation: Scoped search for a public complete facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Add. 4407 (19).
evidence.locator: Cambridge Digital Library, Fragmentarium, general-web, and plausible CUDL IIIF identifier searches
evidence.summary: No exact public facsimile of the complete four-scrap / eight-surface item was found as of 2026-07-15. Plausible guessed CUDL IIIF identifiers returned 404 rather than an exact item record.
notes: This bounded negative finding must be reported as no public copy found, never as proof that the fragments have not been digitized.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Ordering Images, and Cambridge Imaging Services request form.
evidence.locator: request channels, minimum charge, quotation timeframe, and professional imaging service
evidence.summary: Official route to request images online or through photo@lib.cam.ac.uk. The page states a £25 minimum charge excluding VAT and says quotations are normally returned within about two weeks.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Terms and Conditions of Image Supply and Reproduction.
evidence.locator: permitted initial uses and reproduction permission
evidence.summary: Official evidence that supplied images are initially for noncommercial research, private study, or internal evaluation and that publication or reproduction requires prior written permission and a specific licence.

open_questions.question: How narrowly should Havelok's composition date be stated within the late-thirteenth- to early-fourteenth-century debate?
next_steps: Consult G. V. Smithers's 1987 critical edition directly and review the legal, historical, linguistic, and paleographical arguments surveyed by Thomas Liszka.
Retain the broad specialist-repertory end-of-thirteenth-century display until primary scholarly arguments justify narrower bounds.

open_questions.question: Does Cambridge University Library hold an unpublished or differently cataloged complete digital surrogate of Add. 4407 (19)?
next_steps: Ask Cambridge to confirm whether a nonpublic or differently identified digital surrogate exists.
If not, request professional color images of all four physical scraps, all eight surfaces, housing or envelope, edges, and scale or color target where offered.
Record exact image-supply and reproduction licence terms before redistribution.
notes: The negative finding is bounded to a dated scoped search and is not an assertion that the item has never been digitized.

open_questions.question: What item-specific rights statement governs U-M's TEI/OCR encoding layer for AHA2626.0001.001?
next_steps: Recheck the stable U-M item after the Cloudflare interstitial is no longer returned.
Ask U-M Library whether the collection-level public-domain statement extends to TEI markup and bulk XML redistribution.

open_questions.question: What exact reuse licence and fee would Cambridge attach to newly supplied complete-item images?
next_steps: Obtain a written quotation and proposed licence for the complete four-scrap item.
State the intended repository, scholarly-reference, and possible downstream commercial or print-on-demand context explicitly when seeking permission.

review_notes: Primary transmission paths preserve both Skeat's direct Laud work and his scoped dependence on Madden; the graph should not force a single exclusive parent.
Supporting descriptions and the 1911 Cambridge transcription are outside the direct artifact-to-witness path.

All cached supporting-only catalog, metadata, scandata, and scholarship carriers are marked target_work_presence absent. Actual editions, OCR texts, article text, and manuscript images that contain Havelok are marked present with locators.
